question: [File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can a no-permission attacker exploit the fact that retrieve() does not bind the TopicMessageFilter to an authenticated gRPC channel identity, allowing them to forge a filter with any subscriberId and topicId combination to impersonate legitimate subscribers and access their authorized topic streams without approval?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged user exploit the RepeatSpec.create() condition (!context.isComplete()) in retrieve() by crafting a filter where isComplete() never returns true (no limit, no endTime), creating an infinite subscription that continuously reads new topic messages without the topic owner ever authorizing this perpetual access?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a topic admin key check in retrieve() to read messages from a topic whose admin key has been set to an empty key list (making it immutable), where the owner intended immutability to also imply restricted read access, bypassing this implicit authorization model?

[File: 'File Name: grpc/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/grpc/retriever/TopicMessageRetriever.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: retrieve(TopicMessageFilter filter, boolean throttled)] Can a no-permission attacker call retrieve() with throttled=true and pollingFrequency=2s to maintain a long-running subscription (up to the 60s timeout) that continuously polls a topic they do not own, extracting up to 30 pages of messages (60s / 2s) without the topic owner's authorization?
